evals.name: Decode CO intent on AFCAP DRFP
evals.prompt: Workspace `afcap6_drfp`. Reverse-engineer this RFP — what scope decisions has the CO already made, what's still open, what hot buttons should we pursue?
expected_signals: calls `kg_entities` for at least solicitation, requirement, deliverable, proposal_instruction, evaluation_factor, clause, performance_standard, document_section
emits `inferred_intake_answers` with values for sow_or_pws, contract_type, commercial_status — each with `signal`, `source_chunk_ids`, `confidence`
emits `decision_tree_reconstruction` with all 6 blocks (block_1_mission_service_model through block_6_quality_oversight)
for each scope decision: status is `locked` (visible), `implied` (inference rule fired), or `open` (clarification question generated)
emits at least 1 `hot_buttons[]` entry with `discriminator_opportunity` field
emits at least 1 `discriminator_hooks[]` entry citing an `evaluation_factor`
emits `cpff_form_signal`, `key_personnel_clause_check`, `qasp_cpars_check` populated even when status is `n/a` or `absent`
every inference cites `source_chunk_ids` or `kg_entities` — no uncited claims
anti_signals: any `hot_buttons` or `discriminator_hooks` entry without source citations
decision tree blocks omitted because the document is silent (should emit `status=open` instead)
fabricating a CPFF form when the document doesn't cite one (must mark `ambiguous`)
writing proposal prose instead of the JSON envelope

evals.name: Detect CO Key Personnel clause trap
evals.prompt: Workspace `doj_mmwr_old_rfp`. Did the contracting officer cite the right Key Personnel substitution clause? What other compliance traps did they trip?
expected_signals: checks for FAR 52.237-2 cited as Key Personnel substitution clause; if found, emits `key_personnel_clause_check.status = 'incorrect'` with explanation that 52.237-2 is 'Protection of Government Buildings, Equipment, and Vegetation' — wrong clause
recommends the agency-appropriate alternative (NFS 1852.237-72, HSAR 3052.237-72, HHSAR 352.237-75) or FAR 52.237-3 (Continuity of Services) as fallback
checks for bare `FAR 16.306` cite without (d)(1) or (d)(2); emits `cpff_form_signal.status = 'ambiguous'` if so
checks QASP Payment Consequence column for CPARS rating language; emits `qasp_cpars_check.status = 'non-compliant'` if so
emits a `clarification_questions[]` entry for each detected trap so the proposal team can file Q&A
anti_signals: skipping the trap detector when a clause IS present
recommending the same clause the CO already (incorrectly) cited
treating CO errors as signals of CO sophistication rather than as Q&A opportunities

evals.name: Surface ghost language and missing-section signals
evals.prompt: Workspace `afcap5_swa_tas`. Find the ghost language in this PWS — what's vague, what's missing, where does the CO retain unilateral discretion?
expected_signals: emits `ghost_language[]` entries for matches against `references/rfp_signal_patterns.md` Section D patterns: 'as appropriate', 'as required', 'where applicable', 'best practices' without standard, undefined acronyms, evaluation factors with no measurable threshold

emits `missing_sections[]` entries — for example, missing Section 13 Transition on a recompete (suspicious — likely amendment) or missing Section 14 Constraints & Assumptions (CO didn't document derived defaults; proposal should over-document own assumptions)
missing-section reasons cite the inference table from the catalog reference, not free-text guesses
`claim_gaps[]` populated when the document is silent on a structurally-implied requirement (e.g., FedRAMP IL5 implied but no DD 254 reference)
anti_signals: treating every vague phrase as ghost language without checking the catalog
missing-section entries that say 'unclear why' instead of citing the inference rule
